title: Wet-felted nuno cushion panel
subtitle: A 40 x 40 cm nuno-felted front panel for a cushion cover, worked on linen scrim with merino roving inlay
excerpt: Lay merino roving in a pattern on a piece of loose-weave linen scrim and wet-felt them together to produce a textured nuno panel for the front of a 40 x 40 cm cushion cover. The linen puckers and draws as the wool shrinks through it, creating a gathered surface texture.
sourceNotes: Nuno felting (wool-on-fabric bonding) was developed in the 1990s as a textile technique. The combination of merino roving with loose-weave linen or gauze is the standard introductory nuno substrate. Observation-led adaptation for cushion panel construction.

glossaryTerms.definition: A felting technique in which wool fibres are laid on an open-weave fabric and agitated with hot water and soap until the fibres migrate through the fabric weave and lock together on the other side, bonding permanently to the fabric. The fabric is drawn and gathered as the wool shrinks.

A very open-weave fabric used as a base for nuno felting. The loose weave allows wool fibres to migrate through the cloth during felting. Linen, cotton gauze, and silk organza are the most common nuno bases.

content.text: , merino wool fibres migrate through the gaps in an open-weave fabric and lock together on the underside, bonding permanently to the cloth. This panel uses natural linen

as the base: as the merino shrinks, the linen puckers into a gathered, textured surface that is the characteristic nuno look. The pre-felt size is cut to 70 x 70 cm to allow for the 40 to 50 percent shrinkage that produces the 40 x 40 cm finished panel. The panel needs a cotton or linen back panel and a zip to complete the cushion cover; these are sewn by hand or machine after felting.

items.name: Merino roving, 21 to 24 micron, 80 g in your chosen colours
Hot water and olive-oil soap or wool wash
Bamboo rolling mat, at least 80 x 60 cm
Cotton or linen backing fabric, 42 x 42 cm, for completing the cover

content.text: Step 1: Lay the scrim and roving

Lay the linen scrim flat on the rolling mat. Pull wisps of roving 1.5 cm wide and lay them across the scrim in one direction, leaving a 3 cm border of bare scrim around all four edges. Add a second crossing layer of roving at 90 degrees to the first. Work the roving in a design if desired: stripes, a centre motif, or a random scattering all work with nuno. Do not cover the scrim completely; leaving some areas of bare linen visible in the finished piece shows the natural fabric ground.

Pour hot soapy water over the laid roving and scrim. Place the tea towel over the top and press down firmly with your palms. The tea towel protects the roving layers from disturbing as you wet them. Press in circular motions for 3 to 4 minutes until the roving is completely flat and wet through to the scrim.

Rub through the tea towel for 5 minutes, then rub directly on the wet roving surface for 5 minutes. The fibres will begin to migrate through the scrim weave and the surface will feel rougher as they lock. Lift a corner and check the underside: you should see fine wool fibres beginning to poke through the linen weave. This migration is the beginning of the nuno bond.
Step 4: Roll to shrink and texture

Roll the panel up in the mat with the roving side on the outside. Roll back and forth 50 times, applying firm pressure. Unroll, turn the panel 90 degrees, and roll 50 more times. Repeat until the panel has shrunk to approximately 50 x 50 cm and the linen surface shows clear puckering and gathering. Shock in cold water every three roll sequences. Continue until the panel measures 42 to 44 cm square. Lay flat and true up the edges with scissors while damp.

Dry the panel flat. Once dry, sew a cotton or linen backing panel to three sides by hand or machine with right sides facing, insert a cushion pad, and close the fourth side with a hand-stitched slip stitch, or add a zip before assembly for a removable cover.

items.problem: The roving has not migrated through the scrim after extensive rubbing
items.cause: The scrim weave is too tight for the fibre diameter, or the water was not hot enough
items.fix: Use hotter water and rub more vigorously. If the scrim does not allow fibre migration after 20 minutes of rubbing, the weave may be too dense; switch to a looser scrim or silk gauze for the next piece.

items.problem: The panel shrank unevenly and is no longer square
items.cause: Rolling happened mostly in one direction
items.fix: Roll in four different orientations across the process. If the panel has already dried misshapen, wet it again and pull it gently into a square while pinning to a flat board.
